intro_script: Welcome back! It's June 24, 2026, for your daily robotics digest. We've got major M&A news in the Boston Dynamics space, Agility going public, and a look at ASML powering the next chip revolution. Tune in for all eight updates.
outro_script: And that’s your robotics roundup for June 24th, 2026! Looks like even legged bots need a friendly parent. Thanks for tuning into the circuit flow—keep building, and we'll catch you tomorrow!

items.title: Hyundai to Take Full Ownership of Boston Dynamics in $325 Million SoftBank Buyout
items.summary: Hyundai plans to take full ownership of Boston Dynamics through a $325 million buyout from SoftBank. This acquisition will establish Boston Dynamics as a wholly owned subsidiary of Hyundai. Following the purchase, the company intends to pursue a rapid listing on the Nasdaq exchange. The targeted timeframe for this Initial Public Offering (IPO) is by the year 2028.
items.audio_script: From Humanoids Daily, "Hyundai to Take Full Ownership of Boston Dynamics in $325 Million SoftBank Buyout." The report details an agreement wherein Hyundai is set to gain complete ownership of Boston Dynamics through a substantial buyout from SoftBank. This acquisition will result in Boston Dynamics becoming a wholly owned subsidiary under the corporate umbrella of Hyundai Motor Group. Following the completion of this purchase, the company has indicated plans to move toward a rapid listing on the Nasdaq exchange, with an Initial Public Offering targeted for 2028. The development marks a significant consolidation within the robotics sector, indicating increased major corporate investment in advanced humanoid technology going forward.

items.title: Agility to Go Public in $2.5 Billion SPAC Merger with Churchill Capital XI
items.summary: Agility plans an Initial Public Offering through a \$2.5 billion Special Purpose Acquisition Company (SPAC) merger. The transaction involves the participation of veteran dealmaker Michael Klein's SPAC vehicle. This move aims to list Agility on public markets. The company is positioned to capitalize on retail demand for humanoid robots. It is highlighted as the first Western pure-play in this sector.
items.audio_script: From Humanoids Daily, "Agility to Go Public in $2.5 Billion SPAC Merger with Churchill Capital XI." This report details Agility's plan to achieve a public listing through the use of a special purpose acquisition company merger. The company is utilizing a vehicle associated with dealmaker Michael Klein to execute this transaction. The proposed merger valuation stands at two point five billion dollars, facilitating the listing on public exchanges. Industry sources indicate that Agility intends to capitalize on increasing consumer interest in humanoid robotics technology. This positioning marks the firm as the first Western pure-play entity within the burgeoning humanoid sector. This development signals a significant capital influx for the physical robotics domain and warrants close observation from institutional investors tracking automation trends.

items.title: ASML's Giant Machine Powers Future Microchip Revolution
items.summary: ASML, a Dutch company vital to the microchip industry, manufactures advanced lithography machines used to pattern minuscule circuitry onto silicon wafers. These complex machines are essential for creating increasingly powerful and dense chips needed for devices like phones and AI servers. The newest models utilize extreme-ultraviolet (EUV) light to achieve an unprecedented resolution of eight nanometers. Developing these machines required a decade-long, $10 billion R&D effort. The high cost of \$400 million per unit reflects the critical demand from chipmakers racing to improve chip capabilities and sustain Moore's Law.
items.audio_script: From technologyreview.com, "ASML's Giant Machine Powers Future Microchip Revolution." The report details how specialized lithography machines are becoming crucial for developing advanced microprocessors. These complex instruments are necessary to pattern the incredibly small circuits onto silicon wafers that power modern electronics. To achieve higher density and capability in chips used across smartphones and artificial intelligence servers, ASML utilizes extreme-ultraviolet or EUV light technology, achieving a resolution of eight nanometers on its newest models. The development of this machinery represented a decade-long research effort costing ten billion dollars, resulting in units priced at approximately four hundred million dollars each due to sustained industrial demand for capability enhancement. This technological barrier reduction will continue to dictate the pace of advancement within next-generation computing hardware.

items.title: Deep dive into ARM’s physical AI and robotics strategies with Drew Henry
items.summary: Drew Henry provided insights into the development of physical AI and robotics designed for real-world operational limitations. His discussion covered advancements in autonomous systems navigating complex environments. These strategies focus specifically on bridging the gap between advanced artificial intelligence and tangible, physical machines. The topic emphasizes building reliable AI for applications outside controlled settings. This deep dive was initially featured on The Robot Report platform.
items.audio_script: From The Robot Report, "Deep dive into ARM’s physical AI and robotics strategies with Drew Henry." This report details advancements in developing artificial intelligence systems intended for deployment within real-world operational constraints. Specific discussion areas included the progress being made in autonomous systems capable of maneuvering through complex, uncontrolled environments. The methodology discussed centers on bridging the significant gap between sophisticated artificial intelligence models and tangible electromechanical hardware. Furthermore, the segment emphasized the crucial focus on creating reliable AI architectures suitable for applications operating outside strictly controlled laboratory settings. These strategies point toward a maturing integration point between advanced computation and physical robotics. Industry observers anticipate increased practical deployment of robust, outdoor-ready autonomous machines in the near future.

items.title: Why physical AI 2.0 needs a reality check
items.summary: Physical state recovery capability is identified as a crucial element for advancing physical AI. This ability will empower robots to effectively interact with their surrounding environment. The concept was highlighted in an opinion piece originally published on The Robot Report. It suggests that merely focusing on vision and language processing is insufficient for next-generation physical AI systems. Integrating accurate knowledge of the robot's physical state is necessary for practical advancements.
items.audio_script: From The Robot Report, "Why physical AI 2.0 needs a reality check." The piece argues that achieving true advancement in physical artificial intelligence hinges significantly upon developing robust physical state recovery capabilities. This technical ability is posited as the key enabler for robots to achieve meaningful and effective interaction with their operational environments. The analysis suggests that concentrating solely on advancements in vision processing or natural language understanding proves insufficient for next-generation hardware deployments. Therefore, incorporating an accurate, real-time knowledge base regarding the robot's own physical condition is deemed necessary for tangible progress across the field. This focus directs industry development toward integrated systems management moving forward.

items.title: Vention collaborates with FANUC and Universal Robots on software-defined automation
items.summary: Vention is partnering with major industry players, including FANUC and Universal Robots. These collaborations aim to simplify the process for manufacturers designing and deploying industrial and collaborative robots. By focusing on "software-defined automation," Vention seeks to streamline robotic integration. This effort makes advanced robotics more accessible to a wider range of manufacturing clients. The announcement was initially featured on The Robot Report.
items.audio_script: From The Robot Report, "Vention collaborates with FANUC and Universal Robots on software-defined automation." This news details a multi-party partnership designed to simplify the integration of advanced robotics within manufacturing settings. The collaboration involves Vention partnering with established industry leaders like FANUC and Universal Robots. The core methodology centers on developing "software-defined automation," which aims to streamline the entire process for manufacturers designing and subsequently deploying industrial and collaborative robot systems. This joint effort is intended to broaden access to complex robotic capabilities across a wider spectrum of manufacturing clientele. The announcement indicates a notable trend toward software standardization in robotics, suggesting increased usability for smaller production facilities.

items.title: ExRobotics launches UL-certified inspection robot for hazardous environments
items.summary: ExRobotics has launched the ExR-2.5, an inspection robot designed for dangerous settings. This new model carries UL certification, ensuring safe operation within explosive atmospheres across North America. The announcement was initially published on The Robot Report.
items.audio_script: From The Robot Report, "ExRobotics launches UL-certified inspection robot for hazardous environments." The report details the release of a new piece of equipment engineered for high-risk operational areas. ExRobotics introduced the ExR-2.5, an advanced inspection robot specifically designed to navigate and survey dangerous settings. A key aspect of this launch is that the model has attained UL certification, which verifies its safe deployment within explosive atmospheres throughout North America. The technology incorporates specialized safety protocols allowing for operation in environments previously deemed too risky for standard inspection equipment. This development signals a marked advancement in industrial automation capabilities for sectors requiring rigorous safety compliance.

items.title: This flying solar-powered platform could deliver better internet from the air
items.source: MIT Technology Review - AI & Emerging Tech
items.summary: A massive, silver, solar-powered platform is scheduled to traverse the southwestern US and cross the Pacific Ocean to Japan starting in August. This approximately 200-foot-long aircraft was constructed by Sceye, a company located in New Mexico. Upon reaching its destination near the coast of Japan, it will position itself about 18 kilometers above the ocean surface. The platform's deployment aims to improve internet connectivity from an aerial vantage point.
items.audio_script: From MIT Technology Review - AI & Emerging Tech, "This flying solar-powered platform could deliver better internet from the air." The report details a large aerial system designed to boost internet connectivity across significant geographic areas. Developed by Sceye in New Mexico, this massive, silver, solar-powered aircraft is scheduled for a deployment beginning in August. The platform has dimensions of approximately 200 feet in length and is slated to traverse the southwestern United States before crossing the Pacific Ocean en route to Japan. Upon arrival near the Japanese coast, the system intends to maintain an altitude of about 18 kilometers above sea level for its operational duration. This initiative suggests a tangible development pathway for providing high-capacity communications links via airborne infrastructure.

items.title: Verifiable Foundation Models for Robot Safety
items.summary: Deploying large foundation models for robot control is challenging due to their opacity, which complicates formal verification despite their high expressive power. The paper introduces FEARL (Foundation-Enabled Assured Robot Learning), a framework addressing this by modularly decomposing the policy. FEARL separates a powerful Controller ($\text{C}$) from a small Safety module ($\text{S}$). $\text{S}$ operates on low-dimensional sensor observations and bounded context from $\text{C}$, enabling formal verification of critical safety constraints. Evaluation across simulated domains and physical robots demonstrates that this design preserves task capability while ensuring verifiable safety guarantees.

items.title: Engineering Reliable Autonomous Systems: Challenges and Solutions
items.summary: Engineering reliable autonomous systems is a crucial and expanding field in computer science due to increasing system prevalence. A recent workshop report summarizes discussions from the "Engineering Reliable Autonomous Systems" (ERAS) event held in June 2024. The workshop convened experts from formal methods, robotics, industry, and various application sectors. Research focused on three key areas: verification/validation techniques, engineering real-world systems, and safe software architectures. Ultimately, the report provides a roadmap detailing both existing but underutilized solutions and necessary future research directions for the field.

items.title: Enforcing Human-like Kinematics in Dexterous Piano Playing via Adversarial Posture Regularization
items.summary: Reinforcement learning for bimanual piano playing often produces unnatural hand postures with high degrees of freedom. The proposed method, Adversarial Posture Regularization (APR), addresses this by using a small amount of casual human data instead of expensive expert demonstrations. APR incorporates an adversarial objective that forces the trained policy's posture distribution to match a learned human prior. The researchers also collected and released unstructured hand motion data from Meta Quest 3 users playing piano, retargeting it for the Shadow Hand. This approach significantly improves visual quality and adherence to human kinematics across multiple metrics compared to previous techniques.

items.title: Topological Online Learning for Displacement-based Formation Control
items.summary: Introduces Topological Online Learning for Displacement-based (TOLD) control, a real-time framework that adapts interaction topology weights directly to minimize formation distortion. TOLD proposes two methods: Online Gradient Flow (OGF) with unconstrained weights and Online Exponential Gradient Flow (OExpGF) with non-negative convex weights. Theoretical analysis proves OExpGF ensures asymptotic consensus for single-integrator agents on directed graphs, while OGF guarantees bounded distortion. Simulations show significant reductions in Root Mean Distortion Error when TOLD is combined with node-level controllers. Hardware testing using Crazyflie quadrotors confirmed substantial improvements in median formation distortion compared to fixed-weight methods.

items.title: Sim-to-Real Betting on the E-Process: Bringing "simulators" to anytime-valid confidence sequences
items.summary: Combines sim-to-real performance estimation with betting techniques and safe anytime-valid inference. The method utilizes scaled simulators to generate efficient and reliable certificates for estimating a mean value. This approach is particularly beneficial when conducting robot performance testing. The note provides a self-contained account of the construction, minimizing preliminary details by referencing original works. Synthetic examples demonstrating this proposed algorithm are available on GitHub.

items.title: SPACE: Enabling Learning from Cross-Robot Data Toward Generalist Policies
items.summary: Learning generalizable robot policies by scaling data across diverse robots faces challenges because actions are specific to the collecting robot's dynamics. To solve this, a new framework called SPACE uses Cartesian state delta as a universal action representation for all robots. SPACE operates in three domains: varying embodiments, different hardware units of one embodiment, and dynamic changes during operation. The framework comprises a Cartesian state delta policy predicting geometric end-effector displacement and an Action Adapter converting this into robot-specific commands. Experiments demonstrate that SPACE significantly outperforms direct control command prediction across varied robotics setups and maintains robustness to deployment dynamics shifts.

items.title: MinInter: Minimizing Trajectory Interpolation During Data Augmentation for Imitation Learning
items.summary: Imitation learning's data collection costs are addressed by trajectory-level data augmentation, which recombines expert demonstrations with varied initial states. Existing methods often introduce non-expert transitions through interpolation, potentially lowering data quality. Minimizing Interpolation (MinInter) introduces a selection method that chooses the source demonstration requiring the least interpolation for each sampled initial configuration. By explicitly minimizing interpolations during generation, MinInter creates higher-quality synthetic demonstrations compatible with current frameworks. Experiments on 12 tasks demonstrated MinInter consistently improves both data and policy success rates compared to methods like SkillGen.

items.title: DynaWM: Dynamics-Aware Distillation with World Model and Momentum Targets for Smooth Locomotion over Continuous Stairs
items.summary: DynaWM is a novel framework designed to improve bipedal-wheeled robot locomotion over continuous stairs, addressing limitations in current teacher-student control methods. It introduces a world model regularizer to enhance terrain encoding by enforcing forward-dynamics awareness and enabling visualization of terrain geometry. Furthermore, a momentum target encoder stabilizes knowledge transfer by providing consistent distillation targets against non-stationary updates. Analysis shows that the learned representations hierarchically capture terrain geometry with improved adaptability and smoothness. Experiments in both simulation and real hardware confirm DynaWM's superior performance on diverse continuous staircases.

items.title: NavWM: A Unified Navigation World Model for Foresight-Driven Planning
items.summary: Navigating complex environments presents challenges for conventional policies due to myopic decision-making and mode collapse. Existing world models often separate perception, generation, and control components inappropriately. NavWM proposes a unified model integrating latent world reasoning, multimodal action prediction, and controllable visual generation. It utilizes latent world tokens to encode geometric and semantic priors for structural understanding. This framework employs an anchor-based forecasting method generating diverse actions, enabling the model to function as a robust planner using visual foresight.
